bio.notes: EH notes some records give "Eunice Lucy." Source gives "Lucy Eunice."
Named "Isaac Stiles" for maternal grandfather Isaac Stiles Jr. (H00238)

narrative_blocks.header: Husband Titus Frost called father of music
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel's husband Titus Frost was known locally as 'the father of music at North Haven' — the recognized musical leader of the small Connecticut town that had emerged from the colonial Quinnipiac settlement. He also worked as a chairmaker at Muddy River in New Haven, combining the musical reputation with the craftsman trade of an established North Haven householder. The dual identity of musician and chairmaker placed him among the multi-trade artisans typical of post-Revolutionary Connecticut village economies.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven children documented from 1773 marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel and Titus Frost married July 29, 1773 in North Haven Connecticut and had seven documented children — Samuel, Willard 'William,' Polly, Julia, John, Leverett, and Titus Jr. The substantial completed family size was typical of the post-Revolutionary North Haven Stiles-Frost generation, with most of the children remaining in or near the town through their adult lives.

narrative_blocks.header: Born North Haven 1753, died there 1834
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel Stiles Frost was born February 12, 1753 in North Haven Connecticut — the village then still called Quinnipiac — and died there May 1, 1834 at age eighty-one. Her father Isaac Stiles Jr. and her grandmother Esther Hooker connected her to the Stiles-Hooker line that ran continuously through the eighteenth-century North Haven community.

parents.father_research_notes: Ebenezer Frost (1707–~1757) of North Haven CT — non-Hooker, not built.
parents.mother_research_notes: Damaris Ives Frost (1718–1802) of North Haven CT — non-Hooker, not built.

career.notes: Per Frost Family genealogy (BIB007): had 'full charge of the music in St. John's Church at No. Haven, Conn.' from 1783; called 'the father of music' in 1775.
Per Frost Family genealogy: chairmaker at Muddy River; church membership records from 1763 note he made coffins for ten shillings each.

narrative_blocks.header: 'Father of music' at North Haven, 1775
narrative_blocks.body: Church records as early as 1763 note he made coffins for ten shillings each. From 1783 he directed the music at St. John's Church North Haven while working as a chairmaker at Muddy River.

sources.page: Memorial at Montowese Cemetery, North Haven CT

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Henry Merwin Blakeslee and Sophronia Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Frost Blakeslee was daughter of Mabel Stiles Frost and Titus Frost of North Haven; she married Anson Blakeslee in 1810 and bore six children. Two of those children's marriages knit the Hooker descendants of the Isaac Stiles sister-branches back together: Sophronia wed Isaac Lorenzo Stiles, and Lavinia's son Anson later married Mary Cyrilla Stiles.

military_service.notes: Per Frost Family genealogy (BIB007): 'Leverett served in the War of 1812.' No further detail in source.

narrative_blocks.header: Eaton surname traces to New Haven Colony founder
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Stiles Eaton's married surname descended directly from Theophilus Eaton (c.1590-1658) — co-founder of New Haven Colony in 1638 and its first governor. The North Haven branch occasionally spelled the name 'Heaton' on local records but 'Eaton' is canonical for this New Haven-Colony founding family. Through her marriage Elizabeth thus tied the Hooker-line Stiles branch to one of the most prominent New England founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven baptisms, then died at thirty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth bore seven children between 1778 and 1791, all baptized at St. John's Church in North Haven. She died in 1792 at age thirty-seven, less than a year after the seventh baptism. Her husband Timothy Eaton remarried twice — his second wife Catherine Walter died in 1797 after producing one child, and a presumed unnamed third wife produced a son baptized in 1800; the family network shows the high cost of post-Revolutionary childbearing in colonial Connecticut.

parents.father_research_notes: Parents not in source; non-Hooker, not built.

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from New Haven Colony's first governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel Eaton descended from Theophilus Eaton (about 1590-1658) — co-founder of New Haven Colony in 1638 and its first governor for two decades. The Eaton lineage placed her among the small group of Connecticut Hooker descendants who carried New Haven Colony founding-family ancestry alongside their Hartford-Hooker line. The combined Eaton-Hooker descent represented the merger of the two principal seventeenth-century Connecticut colonial-founder lineages.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for aunt Mabel Stiles Frost
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel was given the same first name as her maternal aunt Mabel Stiles Frost — the 'father of music at North Haven' Titus Frost's wife — preserving the family name across the Stiles-Eaton lines of the late-eighteenth-century North Haven community. Her grandmother Mabel Clark Stiles had been the original Mabel; the name passed through three generations of the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Baptized 1783 at St. John's Church North Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel was baptized in 1783 at St. John's Episcopal Church North Haven Connecticut. No birth date was recorded for her or for her siblings — the North Haven family's documentation for this generation depends on baptismal records rather than birth registers. Her death date is not documented in available sources.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother died the year after his birth
narrative_blocks.body: Solomon Eaton was the youngest of his mother Elizabeth Stiles Eaton's seven children; she died in 1792, the year after Solomon's 1791 baptism. He grew up motherless from his earliest infancy — a common eighteenth-century late-childbearing fate for women bearing seven children across two decades of their reproductive years. His father Timothy Eaton was left widowed with the seven young children.

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from New Haven Colony's first governor
narrative_blocks.body: Solomon descended from Theophilus Eaton (about 1590-1658) — co-founder of New Haven Colony in 1638 and its first governor for two decades. The Eaton lineage placed him alongside his elder sister Mabel Eaton among Connecticut Hooker descendants carrying both Hartford-Hooker and New Haven Colony founding-family ancestry. The two principal seventeenth-century Connecticut colonial-founder lineages merged in this branch of the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Baptized 1791 St. John's Church North Haven Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Solomon was baptized in 1791 at St. John's Episcopal Church in North Haven Connecticut. No birth date was recorded for him or for his siblings — the North Haven family's documentation for this generation depends on baptismal records rather than birth registers. His death date is not documented in available sources.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, Connecticut

H00388.prominent_name_reason: John Pierpont (1740-1805) — great-grandfather of J.P. Morgan (1837-1913). Chain: John Pierpont (H00388) → James Pierpont → John Pierpont the poet (1785-1866, abolitionist) → Juliet Pierpont × Junius Spencer Morgan → John Pierpont Morgan. J.P. Morgan's middle name "Pierpont" commemorates this descent from the Pierpont family of New Haven — founded by Rev. James Pierpont, who married Mary Hooker (H00034). Thomas Hooker's great-great-great-granddaughter's grandson became the most powerful financier in American history.

bio.notes: Sources also give surname as "Pierrepont" — alternate spelling

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandfather of J.P. Morgan
narrative_blocks.body: His great-grandson was John Pierpont Morgan (1837-1913), the financier whose name 'J.P. Morgan' carries forward this Pierpont line. Through his son James Pierpont and grandson John Pierpont the poet, the name traveled four generations to one of America's most consequential bankers.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived in old Pierpont mansion, New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Dwight described him as 'a large landholder who lived in the old family-mansion at New Haven.' He had ten children, of whom only four reached adulthood and only three married.

narrative_blocks.header: Family later restored 'Pierrepont' French spelling
narrative_blocks.body: He was born 'Pierpont' but late in his life, around 1802, Hezekiah Beers Pierpont requested the family restore the original French 'Pierrepont' spelling. Hezekiah did not adopt it himself for business reasons; descendants split between the two spellings thereafter.

parents.father_research_notes: Titus Mansfield and Mabel Todd Mansfield of 'Mansfield Farms' New Haven CT. Non-Hooker, not built. Per The Stiles Family in America (BIB008) #452 entry.

bio.bio_blurb: Connecticut-born father of an Illinois migration

narrative_blocks.header: Nine children migrated to Illinois and Michigan
narrative_blocks.body: Of his nine documented children with Sarah Sanford, several joined the westward migration of the 1820s-1840s: Eliza Kelley to Elgin Illinois, Henry to Peoria, John to McHenry County, Edward Sr to Princeville, and Sarah Angeline Andrus to Allegan Michigan. The North Haven Connecticut homestead emptied as the family followed the canal and rail routes west.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his wife died the same week
narrative_blocks.body: Late in December 1868 in Illinois, he and his wife Sarah Sanford died three days apart and were buried together at Princeville — far from the Connecticut where he was born and the Stiles homestead of his mother’s family.

sources.label: WikiTree Mansfield-3 (outreach contact for Mansfield descendants)

narrative_blocks.header: Edward Hooker flagged her marriage date as impossible
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy lists Kezia's marriage to Jesse Mansfield as June 23, 1785, then immediately adds 'there is doubtless an error here as it is not probable they were married at the youthful age of 13.' The corrected year was 1795, when both bride and groom were twenty-two.

military_service.notes: Enlisted September 8, 1818 — three years after the Treaty of Ghent ended the war.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 service began three years late
narrative_blocks.body: Elias enlisted in the Connecticut Militia under Commander Joseph A. Bishop on September 8, 1818, more than three years after the Treaty of Ghent had ended the War of 1812. He is nonetheless recorded on his FindAGrave memorial as a War of 1812 veteran.

narrative_blocks.header: Two infants and one husband in thirty months
narrative_blocks.body: Julia gave birth to twin infants in 1823 who both died the same year, then a surviving daughter Julia Maria in February 1825. Her husband Capt. David Leek died that September at age thirty, leaving her widowed at twenty-eight with one infant.

narrative_blocks.header: His second wife died bearing their only son
narrative_blocks.body: Jesse Merrick married three times across twenty-four years. His second wife Julia Tuttle died fourteen days after delivering their son Howard in 1849; Jesse remarried Catherine Warner the following year and raised Howard alongside the four children of his first marriage and the one from his third.

narrative_blocks.header: Late-life politics: Selectman, Assemblyman, then died
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Clark Stiles served as Selectman of North Haven in 1828 and 1829 and was elected to the Connecticut General Assembly in 1831. He died three years later in June 1834 at age sixty-seven.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety-two, outlived three of her children
narrative_blocks.body: Laura Stiles Mansfield was born in 1787 in Connecticut and died in 1879 at age ninety-one or ninety-two — the longest-lived of her documented Stiles siblings. Of her seven Mansfield children, three died before age fourteen: Mary Elizabeth at thirteen in 1832, Laura Louisa at age two in 1823, and her youngest Laura at age two in 1828.

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters named Laura, both dead at two
narrative_blocks.body: Laura bore seven children with Ebenezer Mansfield. Her fourth child Laura Louisa was born 1821 and died at age two in 1823; five years later in 1826 she named a new daughter simply Laura, and that second Laura also died at age two — in November 1828.

career.notes: Family business — established brick mfg trade that became I. L. Stiles & Son under son Isaac Lorenzo.
Represented N. Haven 1831 per Stiles book #601.

career.organization: US Postal Service, North Haven, Connecticut
St. John's Episcopal Church, North Haven
career.notes: Many years as Warden per Stiles book #601.

narrative_blocks.header: Brickmaker, postmaster, JP, state representative
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Stiles built a multi-role public life in North Haven Connecticut — farmer and brick manufacturer, Justice of the Peace and Postmaster for many years, warden of St. John's Episcopal Church, and representative of North Haven in the Lower House of the Connecticut General Assembly in 1831. He 'held all important town offices' per the Stiles family book — typical of the local-elite figure who structured pre-Civil-War Connecticut town life.

narrative_blocks.header: Two young sons died 1832, probable epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: Two of Isaac Stiles's documented six children — Edward C. and William H. — both died in 1832. The shared death year strongly suggests an epidemic; 1832 was the year of the first North American cholera pandemic that reached Connecticut. Isaac himself died February 4, 1842 at age forty-nine, leaving widow Lois Cooper Stiles to live until 1872 — thirty years past him.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried five of her six children by 1828
narrative_blocks.body: Lucina married Lewis Goodyear in 1811 and bore six children over the next decade. Five died young across three clusters — twin daughters Celestia and Eunice the same day in 1820, infant Francis Lewis in 1822, and Caroline and her sister Lucina nineteen days apart in December 1828.

narrative_blocks.header: Civic offices and an early death at thirty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Horace served as North Haven's Selectman in 1830 and 1831 and was elected to the Connecticut General Assembly in 1837, following his father into local politics. He died in January 1840 at thirty-eight, leaving Lois Pierpont with two infants and forty-seven years of widowhood ahead.

career.organization: St. John's Protestant Episcopal Parish, North Haven
career.notes: Served in this role for over thirty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Three wives, three children buried by 1841
narrative_blocks.body: Ezra outlived his first two wives — Esther in 1836 and Mary in 1853 — and buried three of his five children before they reached age seven. Only Ezra Leander, born 1844 to his third wife, reached adulthood, and he never married.

career.notes: Held various town offices per Stiles book.

I00126.gravestone_inscription: This monument is erected to the memory of the Rev. Isaac Stiles, A.M., who was born in Windsor, July 30th, 1697, received a liberal education at Yale College; ordained to the pastoral office in the church of North Haven, November 11th, 1724, where he served in the ministry 36 years, and died May 14th, 1760, aged 63. ...he preached the gospel with fervor and fidelity. A friend to pure and undefiled religion, with a charitable benevolence to all mankind. Mors Mihi vita Est.

cross_connections.display_label: was father of his first wife, Keziah

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty-six years in North Haven’s pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722, Stiles held the North Haven pastorate for thirty-six years and raised — by his first wife — Ezra Stiles, the Enlightenment polymath who became Yale’s seventh president. His Hooker-descended children came by his second wife, Esther Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He preached against his own melon thief
narrative_blocks.body: Spotting a parishioner stealing his melons during the Sunday intermission, Stiles named him from the afternoon pulpit: “I saw you, John Johnson, thou son of Belial… enter my garden and steal my melons.” His son Ezra called it “rather close preaching, but characteristic of the times.”

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife was the poet’s daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Keziah Taylor, daughter of the metaphysical poet Rev. Edward Taylor of Westfield, died in childbed in December 1727 — twenty-five years old, two years married. Within the year Stiles remarried, to Esther Hooker of Farmington, who bore his ten North Haven children.

narrative_blocks.header: First of his blood liberally educated
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722 — “the first of the name and blood that had a liberal education in America” — he published “A Looking-glass for Changlings” against political turncoats in 1743. His son Ezra became president of the college; his epitaph fills a monument at North Haven.

cross_connections.display_label: Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.
Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.

narrative_blocks.header: Second wife of Rev. Isaac Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Isaac Stiles of North Haven as his second wife. His first wife Keziah Taylor — daughter of the great Puritan poet Rev. Edward Taylor — had died in childbirth on December 4, 1727.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised Ezra Stiles, future Yale president
narrative_blocks.body: She raised her stepson Ezra Stiles from infancy after his birth mother Keziah Taylor's death. He became President of Yale (1778-1795) and one of the most important intellectuals of the American Founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten children with Stiles between 1729 and 1750
narrative_blocks.body: She and Isaac Stiles had ten children of their own between 1729 and 1750. Only Ezra was Keziah's son from the first marriage; all others were Esther's, carrying the Stiles-Hooker line forward through North Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Her name traveled five generations as Esther Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: The name 'Esther Hooker' became a heritable token across five generations of Stiles women — from her (1702) to her granddaughter (1760), and onward through Alice Stiles Mills (1845). Her name carried farther than her lineage.

sources.notes: Samuel Hooker Jr. biography; confirms Plymouth birth, Hartford merchant activity

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Plymouth in his father's transition year
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Plymouth, Massachusetts on May 29, 1661 — where his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had been preaching for several years. The family moved to Farmington that same year, where his father succeeded his brother-in-law Roger Newton as minister.

narrative_blocks.header: His house stood south of the burial ground
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Hooker II had a fine residence at Farmington, next south of the old burial ground — later known as the Mix house — and ran a store for mercantile business in Hartford. He was one of the first of the Farmington Hookers to move from ministry into trade.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a wealthy sea captain's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: In 1687 Samuel Hooker married Mehitabel Hamlin of Middletown, daughter of Capt. Giles Hamlin, a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant. Hooker kept a store and traded out of Hartford while living at Farmington.

career.notes: Mercantile business. Store and fine residence at Farmington.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard; later a fellow of the college. (Julius Gay, Farmington Historical Pamphlets)

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of a sea captain
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Giles Hamlin was a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant of Middletown, Connecticut. Her mother Esther Crow was the daughter of John Crow, merchant of Hartford — placing Mehitabel at the intersection of two of colonial Connecticut's most prominent merchant families.

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of Middletown sea captain Giles Hamlin

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
